evals.prompt: We ran an A/B test for 2 weeks. The primary metric was flat (p=0.4), but click-through to the new feature was up 8% (p=0.02). The team wants to declare the click-through metric the win and ship. Should we?
evals.expected_output: No. This is metric shopping. The primary metric was pre-registered as primary; the click-through was not the success criterion before the test ran. Declaring the secondary metric the win after the fact inflates the false positive rate and produces a portfolio of 'wins' that collectively don't move the business. The honest report: 'primary metric flat; click-through up but underpowered or not pre-registered'. Possible next steps: re-run the test with click-through as the pre-registered primary, or revise the hypothesis to articulate why click-through matters if primary doesn't move.
assertions: Identifies the move as metric shopping
Requires the primary metric to be the one pre-registered before the test
Calls out the false-positive inflation from post-hoc metric selection
Recommends honest reporting (primary flat) over picking the favorable metric
Suggests re-running with click-through as the pre-registered primary if it's genuinely important
Does not approve shipping based on a post-hoc metric switch
References pre-registration as the missing discipline

evals.prompt: We're designing an A/B test. Our metric baseline is 5% (conversion). We want to detect a 2% relative lift. Our weekly traffic is 200k users. How long should we run?
evals.expected_output: Sample size calculation: detecting a relative 2% lift on a 5% baseline (5% -> 5.1%) at 80% power and 95% confidence requires roughly 1.5-2 million users per variant. With 200k/week and 50/50 split (100k per variant per week), that's 15-20 weeks per variant. The practical implication: the team likely cannot afford the calendar time. Options: accept a larger MDE (only detect bigger effects), reduce variance via CUPED or filtering to the relevant population, or run a different test. Recommend stating the affordable MDE explicitly so the experiment's limits are clear.
assertions: Provides a concrete sample size estimate (in the range of millions per variant)
Translates sample size to calendar time given the weekly traffic
Identifies that the test as designed is unaffordable in reasonable time
Suggests CUPED or variance reduction as one alternative
Suggests accepting a larger MDE as another alternative
Suggests filtering to the relevant population as another alternative
Reinforces that the sample size calculation is a gate, not a formality

evals.prompt: Our experiment platform shows a 'real-time results' dashboard. The team checks it daily and stops the test as soon as it shows significance. What's the problem?
evals.expected_output: This is peeking with fixed-horizon frequentist tests. The standard p-value assumes one look at the end of the test. Stopping when significance appears mid-test inflates the false-positive rate dramatically — easily 3-5x the nominal alpha. The 'wins' generated this way are partly noise. Three fixes: (a) pre-register the sample size and look only once at the end, (b) switch to group sequential testing with alpha-spending boundaries, (c) switch to Bayesian or always-valid p-values that are robust to peeking. Most teams choose (a) because it's simplest.
assertions: Identifies peeking as the issue
Explains why peeking inflates false-positive rate
Recommends pre-registering sample size and looking once
Offers group sequential testing as one alternative
Offers Bayesian or always-valid p-values as another alternative
Notes the 'wins' from peeking are partly noise
Recommends a specific fix rather than just diagnosing the problem

evals.prompt: We want to A/B test a new ad placement. Primary metric: ad click-through rate. We expect it to go up. Anything else to consider?
evals.expected_output: Guardrail metrics. Ad click-through is the primary, but the placement could damage other metrics: organic engagement (users distracted by ads spend less time on content), session length, return rate, complaints / negative feedback. Pre-register at least 2-3 guardrails. If a guardrail materially degrades, the CTR win is invalid regardless of significance. Also consider segments — the placement may help one user type and annoy another; pre-register the segments you'll split by (new vs returning, mobile vs desktop). Don't ship based on aggregate CTR alone.
assertions: Recommends guardrail metrics (organic engagement, session length, return rate, complaints)
Pre-registers the guardrails alongside the primary
Notes that a guardrail breach invalidates the primary-metric win
Recommends pre-registering segments for heterogeneous effect analysis
Identifies specific potentially-affected segments (new vs returning, mobile vs desktop)
Does not approve shipping based on CTR alone

evals.prompt: We're running a test where the primary metric is 'revenue per user'. The variance is huge because of a few high-spending users. The MDE we can afford is too large. What can we do?
evals.expected_output: High-variance metrics are the canonical use case for variance reduction. Options: (a) CUPED — adjust each user's revenue by their pre-experiment revenue baseline; for revenue, this typically cuts variance by 50%+, halving the required sample size. (b) Outlier capping / winsorization — cap each user's revenue at the 99th percentile; reduces noise from extreme spenders at a small cost to signal. (c) Filter to a narrower population — exclude high-spending power users whose noise dominates, or restrict to a stratum where variance is lower. Each must be pre-registered. Recommend trying CUPED first as the highest-leverage move.
assertions: Identifies revenue-per-user as a canonical high-variance metric
Recommends CUPED as the highest-leverage variance reduction
Mentions outlier capping (winsorization) as another option
Mentions population filtering or stratification
Requires each technique to be pre-registered
Quantifies the potential improvement (CUPED typically halves variance for engagement metrics)
Does not recommend post-hoc variance reduction (trying covariates until favorable)

evals.prompt: We ran a test and the aggregate result was flat. But when we sliced by user country, three countries showed strong positive effects. Should we ship to those countries?
evals.expected_output: Caution: this is post-hoc segmentation, which is a form of p-hacking unless the country split was pre-registered. With enough segments, some will look positive by chance alone. Two paths: (a) if country was pre-registered as a segment, the result is legitimate and a segment-specific rollout may make sense — apply a multi-comparison correction across the countries to control false-positive rate. (b) if country was not pre-registered, treat this as a hypothesis-generating finding, not a decision-grade result. Run a confirmatory test specifically on those three countries with country pre-registered as the segment. Do NOT ship country-by-country based on the post-hoc slice.
assertions: Identifies post-hoc segmentation as a p-hacking risk
Distinguishes pre-registered vs post-hoc segmentation
If pre-registered, recommends multi-comparison correction across the country tests
If post-hoc, treats as hypothesis-generating, not decision-grade
Recommends a confirmatory test before shipping
Does not approve country-by-country rollout based on the post-hoc slice

evals.prompt: Our team wants to use Bayesian A/B tests because we read it lets you 'stop early when you see a win'. Is that right? What's the catch?
evals.expected_output: Mostly right but partially misleading. Bayesian posteriors are valid at any moment, so stopping early doesn't break the math in the way that frequentist peeking does. But you still need a pre-registered decision criterion (e.g., 'ship when posterior probability of improvement > 95%') and you still need a minimum sample size to make sure the posterior isn't dominated by the prior. Stopping the first time the posterior crosses 95% with very little data inflates the chance that the prior + a small noisy sample looks favorable. The discipline is: pre-register the criterion and the minimum sample, then the early stopping is legitimate. Without those, Bayesian is no safer than frequentist peeking.
assertions: Confirms Bayesian posteriors are valid at any moment
Identifies the need for a pre-registered decision criterion (e.g., 95% posterior probability)
Identifies the need for a minimum sample size
Warns that without a minimum sample, the prior dominates and early stopping is misleading
Concludes Bayesian is only safer than frequentist peeking when paired with pre-registration
Does not endorse stop-the-moment-it-looks-good as a valid Bayesian practice
